---
title: "Study Design"
editor:
  markdown:
    wrap: 72
---

## Overview

The DAWA project is a master protocol comprising several interrelated
studies designed to evaluate antibiotic stewardship interventions in
primary care in Zanzibar, Tanzania.

## DAWA I — Cluster Randomized Controlled Trial

The primary study is a pragmatic three-arm parallel-group cluster
randomized controlled trial (RCT) designed to evaluate the effectiveness
of two intervention packages aimed at reducing antibiotic prescribing in
primary care settings.

**Study setting:** Public dispensaries on Unguja and Pemba islands,
Zanzibar, Tanzania.

**Cluster-level inclusion criteria:**

- Government or government-designated primary care health facilities
  (dispensaries) open to the general public.
- Health facilities with the Zanzibar Electronic Medical Record system
  (ZanEMR) installed in consultation rooms.

**Patient-level inclusion criteria (primary analysis population):**

- Patients of all ages presenting for an acute infectious illness at
  months 4–6 after initiation of the interventions.

**Interventions:**

| Arm | Intervention |
|-----|-------------|
| Arm 1 | ZanEMR-AS + initial group training + supported mentorship + monthly Antibiotic Stewardship Club meetings |
| Arm 2 | ZanEMR-AS + initial group training + supported mentorship |
| Arm 3 (control) | Standard ZanEMR (usual care) |

**ZanEMR-AS** refers to the Zanzibar Electronic Medical Record system
enhanced with antibiotic stewardship features, including:

- Clinical decision support (pop-up guidance at point of care)
- Pre-selected treatment order sets aligned with Zanzibar Standard
  Treatment Guidelines
- Treatment discordance alerts
- AWaRe classification of antibiotics (WHO framework)
- Audit and feedback dashboards

**Antibiotic Stewardship Club:** Monthly peer-based education meetings
at health facilities in Arm 1, where healthcare providers discuss
rational antibiotic prescribing and review local prescribing patterns.

## Primary Outcome

Proportion of encounters with an antibiotic prescribed, assessed at
months 4–6 after initiation of the interventions.

## Key Secondary Outcomes

- Proportion of patients with worse clinical status at day 7 (harm
  outcome)
- Proportion of patients with severe clinical outcome (hospitalization or
  death) by day 7
- Proportion of encounters with an appropriate antibiotic prescribed
- Proportion of antibiotics classified as "Access" according to WHO AWaRe
- Patient-reported additional medication use by day 7

## Sample Size

1,950 patients per arm (5,850 total).

## Randomization

Randomization will be performed at the health facility (cluster) level
with a 1:1:1 ratio, stratified by baseline antibiotic prescription
proportion and health facility attendance rate.

## Blinding

Research assistants enrolling patients and assessing day-7 outcomes will
be blinded to allocation. Patients will not be informed of their
facility's allocation.

## Estimated Timeline

| Milestone | Date |
|-----------|------|
| Protocol finalization | 2025–2026 |
| Ethics approvals | 2026 |
| Estimated first enrolment | 1 November 2026 |

## Nested Studies

**DAWA II — Long-term Outcome:** Following completion of DAWA I,
antibiotic prescribing will be assessed over the subsequent 6 months
(months 6–12) after all active intervention activities have ceased, to
evaluate sustainability of the effect.

**Mixed-method studies:** Semi-structured in-depth interviews and focus
group discussions with healthcare providers and Ministry of Health
stakeholders to assess acceptability and identify barriers and
facilitators of rational antibiotic prescribing.

**DAWA III — Staggered Rollout Evaluation:** If ZanEMR-AS shows
effectiveness in DAWA I, the antibiotic stewardship features will be
rolled out to approximately 30–50 additional primary care facilities on
Unguja and Pemba islands not included in the trial. DAWA III will
evaluate whether the effect generalises to a broader, more heterogeneous
set of facilities under real-world conditions — without stewardship clubs
— using a prospective quasi-experimental **staggered
difference-in-differences (DiD) event study** design. Successive rollout
waves serve as natural experiments, with not-yet-treated facilities acting
as controls for newly treated cohorts. The primary outcome is the monthly
antibiotic prescribing proportion for acute illness. If rollout occurs too
rapidly for meaningful staggering, the analysis reverts to an interrupted
time series (ITS) approach.

**Cost and cost-effectiveness study:** Economic analysis of the
interventions from patient, health facility, and public health system
perspectives.
